data.title: Thermo Fisher: Great Business Still Trading At A Risky Price Before Earnings
data.description:
In July 2025, I published my first and only article about American life science and clinical research company Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO). I rated the stock as a “Hold” and wrote in my conclusion:

Thermo Fisher is certainly a great business with a wide economic moat around its business. However, I see the stock as fairly valued at best, as I don’t see the company growing in the double digits in the years to come. In the last ten years, the acquisition contributed to growth but due to rather high debt levels and very high amounts of goodwill on the balance sheet, I don’t know if Thermo Fisher can continue this strategy. Therefore, I rather see growth rates in the high single digits being reasonable and the stock will remain on my watchlist – together with Waters Corp and Danaher Corporation.

Despite my “Hold” rating and rather cautious stance, the stock increased slightly above 20% in value and clearly outperformed the S&P 500. In the meantime, the stock increased even 50% to $643 and came close to its all-time high once again before the correction drove the stock down again (in line with many other stocks around the world).

In the following article, I will look at Thermo Fisher again before the company will report its first quarter results on April 23, 2026. And while we can argue that Thermo Fisher will perform stably in the coming quarters and economic uncertainty probably won’t have a huge impact on the business, I still consider the stock price too expensive at this point.

Results Fiscal 2025

Let’s start by looking at the full-year results. Total revenue increased from $42,879 million in fiscal 2024 to $44,556 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 3.9% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 5.6% year-over-year from $7,337 million in the previous year to $7,746 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share also increased from $16.53 in fiscal 2024 to $17.74 in fiscal 2025, resulting in 7.3% year-over-year bottom line growth. Only free cash flow declined 13.5% year-over-year from $7,324 million in fiscal 2024 to $6,337 million in fiscal 2025.

When looking at the fourth quarter results, we see even better results. Revenue increased from $11,395 million in Q4/24 to $12,215 million in Q4/25, resulting in 7.2% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 11.9% year-over-year from $2,016 million in the same quarter last year to $2,255 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $4.78 in Q4/24 to $5.21 in Q4/25, resulting in 9.0% year-over-year growth.

Growth: Continuing Solid Pace

Management remains optimistic for fiscal 2026 and issued an optimistic guidance in January 2026. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Turning now to guidance. As Marc outlined, we're initiating a 2026 revenue guidance range of $46.3 billion to $47.2 billion and an adjusted EPS guidance range of $24.22 to $24.80, representing 6% to 8% growth.

The guidance assumes 3% to 4% organic revenue growth, a $300 million revenue tailwind from foreign exchange and 50 basis points of adjusted operating margin expansion. All of this will enable a really strong 6% to 8% growth in adjusted EPS. This guidance is consistent with the financial framing for '26 and '27 that we shared with you back on our Q2 earnings call. It reflects a continued improvement in our organic growth in '26, coupled with very strong earnings growth. The strength of our guidance reflects our industry-leading position, our proven growth strategy and the power of our PPI Business System.

And if the Clario acquisition is closed in mid-2026, expected earnings per share will be about $0.25. During the earnings call, management commented:

The guide, therefore, does not include the benefit of the pending acquisition of Clario.

As a reminder, we expect this deal to close by the middle of 2026. Should that be the case, we would expect $0.20 to $0.25 of incremental adjusted EPS for this year, reflecting the strongly accretive nature of the acquisition.

This would result in revenue growing about 4% to 6% on a reported basis and organic growth being in a range of 3% to 4%. Adjusted earnings per share are expected to grow between 6% and 8.5% (when including the acquisition, growth rates will be between 7% and 9.5%).

Analysts are also expecting earnings per share to grow at a solid pace in the years to come. Analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 9.4% between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2029.

Academic Funding Cuts

In my last article, I already mentioned the problem that the budget cuts by the Trump administration for research and academic institutions will have a negative impact on Thermo Fisher’s business. Especially the end market, Academic & Government, might be affected here, and in fiscal 2025, it was responsible for 14% of total revenue (after 15% in fiscal 2024), and revenue declined in the low single digits in the fourth quarter and full year 2025—mostly due to macroeconomic conditions in the United States and China.

During the last earnings call, management was slightly optimistic that the budget won’t be cut further:

And when I think about the U.S. environment, our assumption here is that there'll be a level of customer caution that will probably abate as the year goes down. But I would still assume in our guidance, it will be a more cautious environment as customers are navigating the landscape. It seems likely that we'll get a flat to slightly up NIH budget. That's going to be a good point in time. Exactly when that happens, TBD. So that should create tailwinds. When I think about over the next couple of years, I would expect that, that will be one of the drivers of us higher in the range. But for now, our assumption is that relatively cautious for 2026.

Unstable World

When looking at the chart above, we also see that the biggest part of revenue is stemming from pharmaceuticals and biotech, and it will be especially important whether these companies will continue spending or maybe cut capital expenditures. And of course, we also must keep in mind that 84% of revenue is stemming from services and consumables. Hence, it is not the decisive question whether companies and academia will buy new instruments, but whether they are continuing to use existing instruments in a similar way as before. And usually, we are not really talking about high levels of cyclicality. Academic research or the R&D spending of pharmaceutical companies is not as recession-sensitive as many other businesses.

Despite the business being rather stable, we should not ignore that the world is very unstable at this point, and the high oil price and high levels of uncertainty might have a huge negative impact on the economy and many businesses in the United States (and other countries around the world). And of course, Thermo Fisher is dependent on the capital expenditures and spending of its customers, who might cut back a little in uncertain times.

On the other hand, especially services and consumables, are essential for pharmaceutical businesses, and it is difficult to reduce spending, as research is extremely important to bring new products to market and generate revenue. Therefore, it seems unlikely for Thermo Fisher to see its revenue decline due to the war between the United States and Iran or higher oil prices.

Growth Strategy

Aside from macroeconomic trends and events, Thermo Fisher continues its growth strategy that consists of 3 pillars: high-impact innovation, the company’s trusted partner status with customers, and the company’s unparalleled commercial engine. And the company will try to grow by using a mix of acquisitions and share buybacks (and it is also paying a dividend):

Let me now turn to capital deployment. We continue to successfully execute our proven capital deployment strategy, which is a combination of strategic M&A and returning capital to shareholders. 2025 was a very active year as we advanced our strategy and added exciting new capabilities that further strengthen our long-term competitive position and create value for all of our stakeholders. During the year, we deployed approximately $16.5 billion, including committing $13 billion to M&A and returning $3.6 billion to shareholders through stock buybacks and dividends.

However, share buybacks contributed only a little bit to bottom-line growth in the past. Since 2018, the company decreased the number of diluted shares outstanding with a CAGR of 0.9%.

While share buybacks contributed only a little bit to bottom-line growth, the constantly improving operating margin contributed a lot to earnings per share growth during the last two decades.

And in the recent past, the company was closing several acquisitions. This is including the completed acquisition of the filtration and separation business from Solventum as well as the acquisition of Clario, which will probably close in 2026. During the last earnings call, management commented:

During the fourth quarter, we also announced a definitive agreement to acquire Clario. The company is a market leader in digital endpoint data solutions, one of the fastest-growing areas and an essential capability in drug development and clinical research. In 2025, the business generated approximately $1.25 billion in revenue. Its differentiated technology and deep medical expertise together enable unique capabilities in generating and delivering digital endpoint data for clinical trials.

And finally, the company expanded the U.S. drug product manufacturing footprint through the acquisition of Sanofi’s fill-finish site in New Jersey.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

One final problem remains—the high valuation multiples Thermo Fisher is trading for. At the time of writing, the stock is trading for 29 times earnings and for 31 times free cash flow. We certainly can make the argument that the stock has been trading for similar valuation multiples in the last ten years, and the stock is more or less in line with the 10-year average P/E and P/FCF ratio.

The problem with simple valuation multiples is always the same: they do not reflect growth rates in any way, and if Thermo Fisher can grow only in the mid-single digits in the years to come, a valuation multiple of 30 is probably too high. However, if the company can grow its bottom line in the double digits (like it has in the past), a valuation multiple of 30 could be justified.

Therefore, we are once again using a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock (which also enables us to calculate a few different scenarios with different assumptions). For every calculation, we are using a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve for every investment) and using 377 million diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number).

As a basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $6,293 million. The company reported a free cash flow margin of 14.1%, which is slightly above the long-term average but seems like a reasonable basis we can use and more or less the margin that was reported in the last 10 years.

Now, we must determine a reasonable growth rate for the years to come. Using a rather cautious assumption, let’s assume 9% growth in the next ten years (in line with analysts’ assumptions) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity (according to CFA recommendations for terminal growth rates). This leads to an intrinsic value of $387.96, and the stock remains clearly overvalued at this point.

We can also make the argument that Thermo Fisher is a high-quality business, and for such a company, we can also calculate with 6% growth till perpetuity.

When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $516.09 for Thermo Fisher, and the stock would be fairly valued. And when looking at previous growth rates, we can also assume double-digit growth rates for the next ten years. In the last ten years, Thermo Fisher grew its bottom line with a CAGR of 13.68%, and in the years before, the 10-year CAGR was even around 20%.

Upcoming Results

On April 23, 2026, Thermo Fisher will report its first quarter results for fiscal 2026, and revenue is expected to be $10.86 billion. Compared to a revenue of $10.36 billion in Q1/25, this would result in about 4.8% growth. Earnings per share are expected to be $3.90 on a GAAP basis, and compared to $3.99 in Q1/25, this would result in a 2.3% year-over-year decline.

It is also worth mentioning that all analysts (17 in total) lowered estimates for the upcoming quarter, and over the last two years, we have rather seen estimates being lowered over time. Considering the lower estimates and the highly unstable economic situation, it seems like a reasonable assumption that Thermo Fisher might lower its guidance for fiscal 2026. However, I do not assume management will lower its guidance as the company is not so dependent on shipping rates or oil prices and has a business that is more or less recession resilient. Nevertheless, we should pay close attention to management’s statements during the earnings call and whether management’s statements are still optimistic or are getting rather cautious.

Conclusion

It seems understandable that investors want a high level of stability and predictability and would rather purchase stocks in companies like Thermo Fisher. But the current price is probably not justified for the stock, and the high price is making Thermo Fisher a rather mediocre investment. We are certainly dealing with a great, high-quality business, but the stock still seems to be a bit too expensive.

data.title: GH Research: WH Decision On Psychedelics To Treat Depression Makes Bull Case
data.description:
"n.b. this post was written prior to market open today - in early trading, the stock has risen further in value, to $24 at the time of writing, up >25%, Current market cap valuation has now risen to $1.47bn."

Investment Overview

The stock of Dublin, Ireland-based biotech GH Research PLC (GHRS) was surging last week, climbing nearly 20% in value, to $18.3 per share, its highest value since April 2022. The stock is now up >40% year-to-date and ~110% on a one-year basis.

GH Research discusses its business as follows in its 2025 annual report / 10-K submission:

We are a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company dedicated to transforming the lives of patients by developing a practice-changing treatment in depression. Our initial focus is on developing our novel and proprietary mebufotenin therapies for the treatment of patients with treatment-resistant depression, or TRD.

Our portfolio currently includes GH001, our proprietary inhalable mebufotenin product candidate and GH002, our proprietary intravenous mebufotenin product candidate. While GH001 is currently delivered via a vaporization device produced by a third party, we are developing a proprietary aerosol delivery device, which is currently in clinical investigation in Europe.

Last week's gains were driven by the news that U.S. President Trump would be signing an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to substances used abroad to treat post-traumatic stress disorder.

According to a White House statement:

Policymakers and the medical field have long struggled to address the burden of suicide and serious mental illness rates in America. Today, over 14 million American adults have a serious mental illness, defined as having a diagnosable mental, behavioral, or emotional disorder that substantially interferes with a person’s life and ability to function, and about 8 million are on prescription medication for these conditions. Suicide rates tragically increased by 37 percent from 2000 to 2018.

Critically, veterans often suffer in greater measure from this tragedy. For over 20 years, there have been more than 6,000 veteran suicides per year, and the current veteran suicide rate is more than twice as much as the non-veteran adult population.

Despite massive Federal investment into researching potential advancements in mental health care and treatment, our medical research system has yet to produce approved therapies that promote enduring improvements in the mental health condition of these most complex patients.

Psychedelic drugs, including ibogaine compounds, show potential in clinical studies to address serious mental illnesses for patients whose conditions persist after completing standard therapy. Indeed, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to specific psychedelic drugs, and there are numerous products currently in the clinical trial pipeline for review of safety and efficacy.

When I last covered GH Research in March 2023, I assigned stock a Hold rating, writing that:

As a speculative, long-term buy and hold I don't dislike GH as an investment opportunity, although it could be several more years before this company can be considered a as a commercial prospect as opposed to an innovative research firm.

At that time, shares were worth $8.7 per share, and they have been somewhat volatile since, typically trading between $5 and $15. In January this year, shares spiked by >40% as the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA") lifted a clinical hold that had been placed on the investigational new drug ("IND") application for GH001, paving the way for the company to run a pivotal Phase 3 study of its candidate.

As such, two years on from my last note, and with stock now up >110% since it was published, this feels like a good time to update on the company and consider whether a rating upgrade is warranted.

GH Research In 2026 - Study Data Suggests Approval May Be In Sight

First, let's take a look at GH Research's product pipeline, as shared in a slide in a recent corporate presentation:

GH Research notes in its 10K filing:

Our lead mebufotenin product candidate, GH001, is formulated for administration via a proprietary inhalation approach. For GH001, we use synthetically developed, pharmaceutical grade mebufotenin free base, manufactured in accordance with current Good Manufacturing Practices, or cGMP, standards.

According to Wikipedia, Mebufotenin is

"found in a wide variety of plant species, and also is secreted by the glands of at least one toad species, the Colorado River toad. Slang terms include Five-methoxy, the power, and Toad venom."

In the U.S., it is currently classed as a Schedule I substance - according to GH Research:

The DEA regulates drug substances and chemical compounds as Schedule I, II, III, IV or V substances. Schedule I substances by definition have a high potential for abuse, have no currently “accepted medical use”.

For any product containing mebufotenin to be available for commercial marketing in the United States, mebufotenin must be rescheduled to Schedule II, III, IV or V, or the DEA must reschedule a specific dosage form or product containing mebufotenin to Schedule II, III, IV or V. Similar rescheduling would be required in the various states and jurisdictions through scheduling-related legislative or administrative action.

This classification has arguably been the biggest obstacle to the approval of psychedelic drugs, but the White House statement from last week notes as follows (and amongst other things):

The Commissioner of Food and Drugs shall provide Commissioner’s National Priority Vouchers to appropriate psychedelic drugs that have received a Breakthrough Therapy designation and are in accordance with the criteria of the National Priority Voucher Program.

The Attorney General shall, in consultation with HHS, initiate and complete review of any product containing a Schedule I substance that has successfully completed Phase 3 clinical trials for a serious mental health disorder, so that rescheduling, if appropriate under 21 U.S.C. 811, may proceed as quickly as practicable for such specific products that are ultimately approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

GH Research has presented the full data set from its Phase 2b study of GH001 at the 2025 American Society of Clinical Psychopharmacology (ASCP) Annual Meeting in Arizona and the 2025 European College of Neuropsychopharmacology (ECNP) Congress in Amsterdam - according to its 2025 earnings press release:

The Phase 2b trial met its primary endpoint with a placebo-adjusted Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) reduction of -15.5 points at Day 8 (p<0.0001). In the double-blind portion, 57.5% of patients on GH001 achieved remission (MADRS ≤10) at Day 8 versus 0% on placebo.

The open-label extension confirmed durable efficacy, with a 73% remission rate at six months achieved with infrequent retreatment visits and no mandated psychotherapy. The median duration of psychoactive experience was ~11 minutes, with 99% of patients deemed discharge-ready within one hour of dosing. Safety was favorable, with no treatment-related serious adverse events and no treatment emergent suicidal intent or behavior.

The study enrolled patients with treatment-resistant depression ("TRD"), and apparently results were significantly superior to treatment with Spravato, an esketamine-derived drug first approved to treat TRD in 2019, in conjunction with an oral antidepressant, before securing approval as a monotherapy in January last year. Spravato, marketed and sold by the Pharma Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), earned ~$1.7bn last year.

As we can see, while it's never entirely fair to compare clinical studies, GH001 appears to have achieved a dramatically larger reduction in MADRS scoring than Spravato, while also achieving a superior rate of remission in patients (57.5% at 8 days, compared to ~10% for Spravato, GH Research says), with a 73% remission rate at six months, and defying the widely held view that patients' remission rates decline significantly with each successive antidepressant treatment failure.

Patients in the GH001 study also required an average of four treatment visits in 6 months, while Spravato patients complete ~23 visits over the same period (GH Research says, citing Institute for Clinical and Economic Review data). From a safety perspective, there were no treatment-related serious adverse events in the study and no treatment-emergent adverse events involving suicidal intent or behavior. In the open-label extension ("OLE") part of the study, GH Research notes:

MADRS reduction in the Placebo group following first active treatments after entering the OLE, was comparable to the results observed in the GH001 group in the DB part

As we can see in the slide above, GH Research is now confident that it has a "best-in-class" TRD therapeutic treatment, a best-in-class psychedelic, and a best-in-class molecule, and the company believes that, should GH001 be approved, it would qualify for five years' exclusivity in the U.S. (i.e., no generic versions for GH001 could be launched by competitors) and ten years in Europe if approved in that territory, while the product would also be patent protected, including its novel use as an inhalable product, its aerosol composition, and as a medical device.

Looking Ahead - Critical Phase 3 Study Dovetailing With Changing Attitudes

As of the end of 2025, GH Research reported a cash position of $281m, having completed a ~$150m funding round last year. R&D expenses for 2025 amounted to $38.8m, and G&A expenses were $22m. Net loss amounted to $(48.3m), or $(0.79) per share.

Discussing the clinical hold in its 2025 10-K filing, GH Research states:

in September 2023, our IND for GH001 was placed on a clinical hold by the FDA requiring an inhalation toxicology study in a non-rodent species and an additional inhalation toxicology study. We completed the toxicology studies and met with the FDA, and the clinical hold was lifted by the FDA in December 2025.

In summary, then, it seems as though the path to a full approval of GH001 is beginning to open up. The clinical hold has been lifted, and the U.S. government has expressed a willingness to consider fast-tracking approvals for psychedelic drugs and reclassifying them from Schedule I to make it easier to manufacture, transport, and distribute the therapy.

The equation therefore seems simple: succeed in its Phase 3 pivotal study, and with a Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher ("CNPV"), GH001 could be on the market a few months after its New Drug Application requesting commercial approval is submitted to the FDA (assuming it is accepted).

With its strong efficacy and safety profile and ability to restrict patient treatment visits, we may speculate that the therapy could take a significant amount of market share from existing standards of care, including Spravato, antipsychotic medicines Abilify (aripiprazole) and Rexulti (brexpiprazole), which earned a combined ~$1bn last year, and second-generation antipsychotic medications Seroquel (quetiapine) and Zyprexa (olanzapine).

GH Research's market cap valuation has risen above $1bn and currently stands at $1.14bn, which, in my view, may be a significant undervaluation of a company that may be one positive data set away from pushing for approval in a multi-billion dollar market, with a best-in-class safety and efficacy profile. I would speculate that GH001 could earn ~$500m - $1bn in peak annual revenues (perhaps more), and therefore, using a 3x forward price to peak sales ratio, I'd speculate GH Research merits a $1.5bn - $3bn market cap valuation today.

There are naturally risks to consider. For example, while the current government in the U.S. is taking steps to make it easier for psychedelic drugs to be approved to treat depression in the U.S., there is no guarantee that any legislation will be upheld by the next administration. Also, in terms of competition, GH Research notes:

Several biopharmaceutical companies have therapies, including other tryptamine compounds, in preclinical and clinical development being evaluated or planned to be evaluated in mental illness, including in TRD patients, including AtaiBeckley (ATAI), Axsome Therapeutics (AXSM), COMPASS Pathways (CMPS), Helus Pharma and Definium Therapeutics.

Of the programs with other tryptamine compounds, the most advanced is COMPASS Pathways’ investigational therapy COMP360 given in conjunction with psychological support, for which results of a Phase 2b trial in TRD have been reported and a Phase 3 program has been initiated.

Compass stock was also soaring last week, gaining ~13.5%, and the company has attained a market cap valuation of ~$640m, but its data seems somewhat less impressive than GH Research, in my view. Covering the company for Seeking Alpha in February this year (assigning a Hold rating – stock is down ~13% since), I noted its candidate COMP006, a psilocybin-based therapy, achieved a clinically meaningful difference of -3.8 points vs. placebo in MADRS scoring - data for GH0001 showed a >15 point reduction, as discussed above.

All things considered, then, and as noted above, the obstacles to GH001 securing approval in the U.S. in TRD appear to be being removed one by one - drug classification, clinical holds, lengthy review periods, positive data sets, funding, alignment with the FDA, and so on. Furthermore, approval in Europe may also be achievable, and there are also studies ongoing in postpartum depression and bipolar disorder, plus a second generation product, GH002 (mebufotenin for IV administration), now also in clinical studies.

While acknowledging some risks to the bull thesis – an unsuccessful pivotal study, numerous competitors, and the U.S. government not following up on its promise to fast-track approvals and reclassify drug products - I'd still make GH Research a solid Buy opportunity at this time, although given the recent gains in the share price and the long-ish wait for pivotal data (it took around two years for the Phase 2b study to complete and data to be shared, by my estimation), I'd also suggest there may be opportunities to buy stock at a cheaper price in the coming months.

Longer term, however, as discussed above, I'd speculate the true valuation of this company could be somewhere between $1.5bn and $3bn, given the exciting opportunity in play.

data.title: ICICI Bank: Solid Q4 Results Should Support Recovery From Recent Over-Correction
data.description:
ICICI Bank (IBN) is currently in the middle of a recovery after a sharp fall in March. Positive quarterly numbers, easing of the West Asia crisis, and some regulatory relief should give it some short-term tailwind that can be bought into for 10% or so short-term gain, even though the longer picture remains slightly unclear.

The 2025 Drift and March (Over)correction

ICICI Bank touched all-time high levels of just under $34 and stayed there for May, June, and July 2025, driven by a two-year-long golden era of peak net interest margins (NIM) with high-interest loans and low-interest deposits. The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) had a major rate-hike cycle from May 2022 to Feb 2023 (going from 4% to 6.5%). This made 2023 and 2024 a “sweet spot” where banks lent at repo but had to pay out fixed deposits on older rates. But eventually, the deposits had to be renewed at higher rates, and at the same time, the post-COVID hyper demand for credit started normalizing (loan growth went from 18-20% to 12-14%), while bank deposits faced stiff competition from mutual funds, who were grabbing an increasingly larger share of Indians’ savings—this led to an era of NIM compression, which dipped from a peak of 4.78% in Q1 FY2024 to 4.27% in Q2 FY2025. Analysts feared it might eventually revert to 4.0% levels, but it seemed to have stuck at those levels, ending Q3 FY2026 at 4.3%.

2025 also saw massive FII outflows from the Indian market due to rising US yields, with ICICI Bank being one of their main holdings. For ADR holders, the pain was increased by the poor performance of the Indian Rupee, causing it to drop sharply towards $25 in March. At the same time, the RBI increased capital requirements for banks on unsecured retail lending, creating an effective tax on ICICI’s most profitable segment. Then in early 2026, the RBI further tightened the regulatory noose and prohibited banks from lending to prop trading and required 100% collateral for lending to brokers and ending the automatic bundling of insurance with loans. The spiking West Asia conflict, coupled with regulatory tightening, led to massive bearish positions, with put activity hitting record highs. The usual put-call ratio of 0.8-1 spiked to 1.68 when the stock approached its 52-week low in the Indian markets, with record open interest levels.

The April Recovery and the Results

Since hitting bottom in March, the parent stock has staged a sharp 11% recovery in the Indian markets. The RBI firstly provided relief by deferring the capital requirement norms for brokers until July 2026. As the anxiety around the West Asia crisis started to ease, the open interest unwound with the put-call standing at an optimistic 0.52 (as of 4/19).

In between this relief rally, ICICI has posted solid Q4 2026 results, and the board has recommended an INR 12 dividend (a yield of around 0.64% for ADR holders after taxes). NIM has marginally ticked up to 4.32%, now solidifying this level as the new hopeful standard and seemingly avoiding the worst-case scenario of 4.0%. Y-o-Y, the bank has seen a loan growth of 15.8%. This is despite sluggish retail growth, with most of the new growth coming from Business Banking and Rural Portfolio (both contributing approx. 25%). While some caution lingers over the slowness of ICICI’s traditional stronghold of retail banking, this does demonstrate a healthy capacity to pivot—with their dominance in rural banking making the best use of the government’s mandatory guidelines to lend to priority sectors (mostly agriculture). This also introduced the monsoon outlook as a sentiment factor for the bank, which so far has been neutral.

A much talked about number is also the dramatic reduction in provisioning from INR 2,556 crore to INR 96 crore, a reduction of 96%. This was partly because of a one-time provision of INR 1,283 crore in the previous quarter due to an RBI mandate. But the rest is still very impressive, highlighting massive recoveries and upgrades (to the tune of INR 3,068 crore) backing ICICI’s reputation for a very healthy loan book, as well as the INR 13,100 crore contingency buffer that the bank is sitting on. This has also provided a near 3% boost to the bottom line, taking it to 8.5% even as the core operating profit grew at a more modest 5%.

Price Targets

The Q4 results aren’t transformative—but they are highly reassuring to what seems to be an oversold stock and are helping immensely in changing the narrative around it after it took a beating in March. A massive leadership shock and resulting uncertainty at one of its biggest rivals, HDFC Bank, also makes ICICI look good by comparison to returning investors. ICICI trades at 3x its book value, at a book value per share (BVPS) of INR 480 (calculated from the results). With 15.8% loan growth, the BVPS goes up to INR 550, which, at the 3x multiple, translates to a target price of INR 1,650, maintaining the 3x multiple. For the ICICI Bank ADR on the NYSE, this would translate to a price target of roughly $35, which is the peak level it had seen in mid-2025.

Now I’m not really the biggest fan of trying to “quant up” predictions based on quarterly results, but what I think is the important part here is that this is what the market is likely thinking right now. After a year-long drift and a steep crash in March, the narrative has changed to this being a stock that is, in theory, ready to go back to its relatively recent peak. This is a pretty strong momentum for a stock that has already bounced from $25 levels to $28 levels. What I’d really like to do here is to get in on the bounce right now and hold the stock until $32-33 levels for a 10-12% upside in the near term. The stock has not yet reacted to the results: if it goes up, ride the wave and get out quickly; if there is a bit of ‘sell the news,’ buy the dip and hold it for the quarter. Any further easing on the West Asia crisis should keep this propped up. After March, the downside also looks limited.

Risks and Conclusion

In the long term, ICICI is one of India’s largest banking giants that’s likely not going anywhere and can be held on to for some diversification of modest growth. For the short-term play I outlined, however, the West Asia crisis remains an ever-present unpredictable risk in the short term. Moreover, its medium-term outlook looks modest at best, as the RBI seems to be taking a highly dovish stand even as Indian consumers seem increasingly disillusioned with the idea of 6% returns that barely seem to be keeping up with the actual cost of living and increasing aspirations for consumption. Any RBI relaxation on consumer credit or lending for trading should provide relief and a rally in the current environment, but, barring such unexpected relief, ICICI still has some work to do to figure out where its growth will come from over the next half-decade.
